header.title: How to Never Lose Tic Tac Toe Again
header.excerpt:
Tic Tac Toe is a solved game, thus it has a known optimal strategy.
With perfect play from both sides, the game ends in a draw.
That means you can memorize a compact set of practical rules and never lose!

In this blog I explain how I discovered it, and you'll find out how a perfect player thinks through an interactive and intuitive demo.

data.content:
In the unforgettable year 2020, when I was in high school, I wrote a terminal game in C++ for Tris (Tic Tac Toe). At the time, I was just trying to build a fun game and make the CPU hard to beat.

I did not know formal AI terms yet, and I definitely did not know alpha-beta pruning.

What I built anyway was a full recursive game-tree solver with backtracking.

I had a class called Solver with an almost magical method: bestMove , which took as input a tic-tac-toe board and returned the position where to put the symbol to make the best move possible. But… the method’s body? I had no idea on how to compute it. I thought about how I usually play this game: think what the opponent will do after my move.

So, it came to my mind the most absurdly working idea of that week: simply try a move and call bestMove simulating an opponent (a new Solver instance). If that move leads to a win for the original Solver that’s it: the best move. Otherwise, try other moves and simulate a perfect opponent with the same method bestMove every time.

That’s how I invented the Minimax method for classical adversarial search, unknowingly:

  1. Try a move.

  2. Assume the opponent also plays the best move.

  3. Continue recursively until terminal states.

  4. Backtrack and compare outcomes.

  5. Return the best possible move.

So this project is both nostalgia and a clean, explainable AI showcase.

You can go directly to the demo here: https://tris.bestplayer.dev/.

The motivation

I enjoy games, and I am very competitive.

Tic Tac Toe is simple, but that is exactly why it is perfect for showing decision-making:

  • The state space is small enough to fully inspect.

  • The game is solved.

  • Every move can be justified with concrete outcomes.

This makes it ideal for a portfolio demo where you can see the algorithm think.

The original CPU modes

The live demo includes the original three opponent levels i designed when I first built this game. Each one intentionally exposes a different level of reasoning.

Easy mode

The CPU picks a random free cell.

That’s it. It’s like asking someone who doesn’t know how to win.

Medium mode

The CPU is still mostly random (like in easy mode), except for one tactical rule: if the player has an immediate winning threat (two in line and one free), block it.

That’s like asking someone who doesn’t want you to win, but can be tricked to lose.

Difficult mode

This is the advanced mode, the magical method to find the best move: full game tree exploration and recursive minimax-style evaluation with backtracking from leaves to root.

For each legal move, the solver computes whether that branch leads to forced win, draw, or loss, assuming perfect play from both sides.

In other words, it’s like asking someone who can travel in the multiverse to see which move will lead to the universe where they’ll not lose. If you are not familiar with that terminology, I suggest you to watch Marvel’s films (or study theoretical physics).

For a Human, what is the Strategy?

Tic Tac Toe has a known optimal strategy. With perfect play from both sides, the game ends in a draw.

That means you can memorize a compact set of practical rules and never lose.

Simple Version

  • Take the center when available.

  • If center is taken, prioritize corners.

  • Block immediate threats first.

  • Watch for forks (positions creating two simultaneous threats).

If you prefer a more detailed tutorial, here it is.

Note that you may have to reason a bit about symmetry when playing against someone else, especially if they start as first player. For this tutorial you will be the red X, and the opponent will be the blue O.

If You Start First

In this case, you want to start in a corner. Without loss of generality, thanks to symmetry, this will always lead to this situation.

After that, if the opponent plays anything other than the center you can always find a side where to leave the middle empty and the two corners occupied by the X, like this.

Then, the opponent is forced to play the O in between the two X, and you can always find a new free corner to put the third X and create a fork:

Any move from the opponent will still lead you to win this game.

However, if the opponent plays in the center after your first move, you’ll probably need to force a draw. Play the next X on one of the sides opposite to your corner:

From there, it’s pretty easy to force a draw, by simply blocking the threats.

If Opponent Starts First

In this case, you still want to recall some moves from the “You Start First” section, as a warning to avoid some mistakes by simply swapping the POV. However, there are still a few “forced” moves that will help you win or, in the worst case, force a draw.

If the opponent plays in the center or in one corner: place the X in a corner or in the center respectively, like this.

Then, follow the opponent’s moves to force a draw.

If the opponent places the O in one of the sides, you have a possibility to win. First: place the X in the center.

Then, if the opponent plays in the opposite side, just take another side with the X.

In this way, after the forced O in the last free side to stop you from winning, you can fork by playing in one of the adjacent corner to your two Xs:

It can happen that the opponent will not place the O in the opposite side as second move. If that’s the case, it’s still pretty easy to force a draw.

Demo and What to Look At

Open the demo: https://tris.bestplayer.dev/.

When CPU difficult mode is active, you can inspect the tree explorer and see:

  • candidate moves from current position,

  • color-coded outcomes (win/draw/loss),

  • number of explored nodes,

  • and the final move selected by the solver.

This turns a black box move (the “magical method”) into a transparent decision process.

Personal Note

I built this because, after recently playing tic-tac-toe with a friend, I wanted to reconnect with a project that came from pure curiosity.

The title is playful, yet the core idea is a small, concrete demonstration of concepts that scale to bigger AI systems:

  • state representation,

  • transition modeling,

  • recursive search,

  • utility propagation,

  • opponent modeling,

  • explainability through intermediate evaluations.

In larger games, exhaustive search becomes impossible and we need pruning, heuristics, and approximations, but the mental model starts here.

Back then I had no formal framework, just persistence and a lot of trial and error and fun ideas.

Now, studying Artificial Intelligence in MSc, I can name the method, explain it rigorously, and still keep the same spirit: build, test, reason and improve.

Also yes, I still hate losing.
